graphNode.label: TPU (Tensor Processing Unit)
graphNode.def: Google's custom application-specific integrated circuits (ASICs) designed to accelerate machine learning workloads, increasingly specialized for both training large models and serving fast agentic inference.

graphNode.label: AI Inference Chip
graphNode.def: Specialized silicon designed to run AI model inference at high speed and low latency, particularly to support responsive agentic workflows requiring rapid multi-step reasoning and execution.

graphNode.label: Purpose-Built AI Infrastructure
graphNode.def: The full-stack design philosophy of co-optimizing custom silicon, networking, data centers, and energy systems specifically for AI workloads rather than repurposing general-purpose computing infrastructure.

graphNode.label: Data Residency
graphNode.def: The requirement that data be stored and processed within a specified geographic or jurisdictional boundary, increasingly relevant for enterprise AI deployments in regulated industries.

graphNode.label: AI-Speed Cyber Defense
graphNode.def: A strategic framing — used in executive and business leadership contexts (e.g., HBR, enterprise security discourse) — for the proposition that defensive cybersecurity operations must match or exceed the tempo at which AI-assisted offensive attacks can be launched and iterated. The concept implies that traditional human-paced security operations centers (SOCs) and response workflows are structurally outpaced by AI-accelerated threat actors, requiring AI-native defensive tooling and automated response.

refs.q: “Cyber Defense Has to Move at the Speed of AI” Harvard Business Review
Google stopped a zero-day hack that it says was developed with AI The Verge — AI

proposals.reason: 2 sources, 2 independent (hbr.org + theverge.com), 3d on longlist. Trusted: hbr.org.

graphNode.label: Agentic Era
graphNode.def: An industry framing — most prominently used by Google ("the agentic era") and adopted by other lab marketing — for the current or emerging phase of AI development centered on widespread deployment of autonomous, multi-step agents. The framing is contested: critics (notably the AI agent reliability literature) argue it asserts a periodization that outpaces measured capability, while proponents argue agentic deployment represents a genuine workflow-integration shift distinct from prior single-turn patterns.

refs.q: The latest AI news we announced in April 2026 Google AI Blog
An Interview with Ben Thompson at the MoffettNathanson Media, Internet & Communications Conference Stratechery
OpenAI keeps shuffling its executives in bid to win AI agent battle The Verge — AI

proposals.reason: 3 sources, 3 independent (blog.google + stratechery.com + theverge.com), 11d on longlist. Trusted: stratechery.com.

graphNode.label: Privacy by Design
graphNode.def: An architectural principle applied in AI deployment contexts where sensitive data — such as proprietary CAD geometry — must not leave a controlled environment. In this framing, on-premise model hosting (e.g., running open-weight models on local GPU hardware) is treated not as a performance optimisation but as a compliance and confidentiality requirement. Distinct from the broader regulatory usage of the term in GDPR contexts, though related.

graphNode.label: Observability (ML Infrastructure)
graphNode.def: The practice of monitoring, visualising, and diagnosing the health and performance of large-scale machine learning infrastructure — spanning cluster hardware, network utilisation, and model training runs. In foundation model contexts, typically implemented via tools such as Prometheus (metrics collection) and Grafana (visualisation/alerting), positioned as a cross-cutting operational layer above hardware, orchestration, and ML framework tiers.

graphNode.label: Hyperscale Data Center
graphNode.def: A very large-scale data center facility — typically tens of thousands of acres or gigawatts of power capacity — built to support cloud and AI compute at massive scale. In the AI context, hyperscale data centers are the physical substrate for frontier model training and inference infrastructure. The term is used by operators, planners, and critics alike, though 'hyperscale' has no universal threshold; usage ranges from technical capacity descriptors to policy shorthand for facilities whose energy demands rival those of entire states.

refs.q: All the latest updates on AI data centers The Verge — AI
Data Center Discontent, Understanding the Opposition, Fixing the Problem Stratechery

proposals.reason: 2 sources, 2 independent (theverge.com + stratechery.com), 7d on longlist. Trusted: stratechery.com.

graphNode.label: AI Energy Demand
graphNode.def: The aggregate electricity consumption attributable to AI compute infrastructure — primarily data centers running training and inference workloads. The term appears in policy, environmental, and utility discourse as a framing for how AI expansion strains power grids and raises residential energy bills. It is contested in scope: industry characterises data center energy growth as manageable and offset by efficiency gains, while critics and regulators (including the NAACP, bipartisan senators, and Pew survey respondents) frame it as an inequitably distributed burden on communities and ratepayers.

refs.q: All the latest updates on AI data centers The Verge — AI
Data Center Discontent, Understanding the Opposition, Fixing the Problem Stratechery

proposals.reason: 2 sources, 2 independent (theverge.com + stratechery.com), 7d on longlist. Trusted: stratechery.com.

graphNode.label: Ratepayer Protection Pledge
graphNode.def: A voluntary commitment signed by major tech companies, referenced in a Senate letter to the EIA, to protect electricity ratepayers from cost increases attributable to data center expansion. The pledge is cited by Senators Warren and Hawley as a benchmark against which mandatory energy-use disclosures would hold signatories accountable. Its voluntary nature is precisely what critics say warrants mandatory reporting requirements to verify compliance.
